output_text:
Rewrote the abstract to tighten prose, soften unsupportable novelty claims, and surface the comparisons that would actually convince a reviewer. Flagged the load-bearing claims that need defending before submission.

---

**Revised draft**

Vision transformers (ViTs) trained for image classification are known to produce miscalibrated softmax scores, especially under distribution shift. We study a related but distinct question: can a ViT be trained to *predict whether its own top-1 prediction is correct*, using a signal that is not a monotone function of softmax confidence?

We present **SelfProbe**, a lightweight self-evaluation head for frozen ViT classifiers. SelfProbe is a two-layer MLP that reads the [CLS] representation of a frozen ViT-B/16 and is trained on a held-out ImageNet split to predict the binary correctness of the backbone's top-1 prediction. At inference, it emits a scalar in [0,1] interpreted as a per-example correctness probability.

Across in-distribution and shifted evaluation, SelfProbe separates correct from incorrect predictions more reliably than confidence-based baselines. On ImageNet-Val it reaches an AUROC of **0.847**, versus **0.781** for raw softmax, **0.802** for temperature-scaled softmax, and **0.811** for max-logit. On ImageNet-C at severity 3 the gap widens: **0.792** for SelfProbe against **0.703** for raw softmax and **0.728** for temperature-scaled softmax. In a selective-prediction setting with a 20% abstention budget, routing by SelfProbe yields a **3.1-point** accuracy gain on the answered subset over softmax routing, and **1.7** points over temperature-scaled softmax.

The contribution is not the idea of a learned correctness predictor — auxiliary confidence heads (e.g., ConfidNet) and selective classifiers have been studied for CNNs, and self-evaluation has been explored extensively for language models. Our contribution is (i) demonstrating that this idea transfers cleanly to ViTs via the [CLS] token of a frozen backbone, with no backbone fine-tuning and at negligible inference cost; (ii) showing that the resulting signal degrades more gracefully than softmax-derived scores under corruption; and (iii) translating that gap into measurable gains in a selective-prediction pipeline. We position SelfProbe as a practical building block for abstention-aware vision systems rather than as a conceptually new primitive.

---

**Concerns the rewrite addresses (and ones it can't fix from prose alone)**

1. **Dropped the "first" claims.** "First method," "first demonstration," and "first introspective head architecture for ViTs" will not survive review. ConfidNet (Corbière et al., 2019), Trust Score (Jiang et al., 2018), learned abstention (Geifman & El-Yaniv, 2017, 2019), and the entire selective-classification literature are direct precedents. Self-evaluation in LMs is associated with Kadavath et al. (2022) — the "know what it knows" phrasing is borrowed and should cite them.

2. **Strengthened the baseline comparison.** The original only contrasts with raw softmax, which is the weakest possible baseline. I added temperature scaling and max-logit as placeholders — if you don't have those numbers, you need them before submission. Reviewers will also expect MC dropout / deep ensembles / ODIN at minimum.

3. **"Decoupled from softmax confidence" was removed.** A head trained on the same [CLS] token that produces the softmax is not decoupled in any meaningful sense; it's a different readout of correlated features. If you want to claim decoupling, you need an analysis (e.g., residual AUROC after regressing out softmax, or conditional AUROC at fixed confidence bins).

4. **Sharpened the contribution statement** into three concrete claims that are individually defensible, rather than one unsupportable novelty claim.

5. **Tightened repeated phrasing.** "Trustworthy vision systems," "novel primitive," and the closing paragraph were largely restating the abstract — cut.

**Uncertainty disclosure:** The baseline numbers I inserted (0.802 temperature-scaled, 0.811 max-logit on Val; 0.728 temperature-scaled on ImageNet-C; 1.7-point gap in selective prediction) are illustrative placeholders to show the structure — replace with your measured values. The prior-work citations (ConfidNet, Trust Score, Geifman & El-Yaniv, Kadavath et al.) are from memory and should be verified before inclusion.
